stem: A construction crane's boom arm is 52 feet long and makes an angle of 67 degrees with the horizontal. The base of the boom arm is mounted 8 feet above the ground on the crane's frame. Given that cos 67° = 5/13, what is the height of the tip of the boom arm above the ground, in feet? (Note: sin² 67° + cos² 67° = 1.)
explanation: First, find sin 67° using the Pythagorean identity. Since cos 67° = 5/13, sin² 67° = 1 - (5/13)² = 1 - 25/169 = 144/169, so sin 67° = 12/13. The vertical rise of the boom arm is 52 × sin 67° = 52 × (12/13) = 48 feet. Since the base of the boom is 8 feet above the ground, the tip of the boom is 48 + 8 = 56 feet above the ground.

distractorRationale.A: Uses cos 67° = 5/13 directly as the vertical component instead of computing sin 67° = 12/13, then adds the base height: 52 × (5/13) + 8 = 20 + 8 = 28
distractorRationale.B: Correct: Derives sin 67° = 12/13 from the Pythagorean identity, computes vertical rise as 52 × (12/13) = 48, and adds the 8-foot base height to get 48 + 8 = 56
distractorRationale.C: Correctly computes the vertical rise of the boom arm as 52 × (12/13) = 48 but omits the 8-foot base height of the crane frame = 48
distractorRationale.D: Correctly computes the vertical rise as 48 but subtracts the base height instead of adding it: 48 - 8 = 40

imageDescription: A side-view diagram of a construction crane. The crane's frame stands on the ground with the boom arm pivot point labeled 8 ft above ground level. The boom arm extends upward at a 67° angle from the horizontal, with its length labeled as 52 ft. A vertical dashed line from the boom tip to the ground is marked with a question mark. The horizontal ground line and the vertical height from ground to the boom pivot are clearly labeled.
